prompt: A developer asks a model to 'write some tests for my function' but pastes no code. Why does this so often produce useless output?
choices: The model only sees the text you send, so without the code and framework it invents a signature and guesses the tests
Longer prompts always produce more accurate tests regardless of content
The model reads your repository and CI config automatically
Wording only affects tone, never which framework or cases the model chooses

prompt: Which statements about the system (developer) message versus the user message are correct? Select all that apply.
choices: Durable rules like the test framework and coding standards belong in the system message and persist across turns
The specific request for this function or this user story belongs in the user message
The user message takes priority over the system message
Safety policy and persona are best placed in the system message

prompt: When prompting a model to refactor a function, why wrap the existing code in delimiters such as triple backticks or XML-style tags?
choices: To separate your instructions from the code the model should process, so a comment in the code isn't read as an instruction
To force the model to use a higher temperature
To translate the code into another language automatically

prompt: A Business Analyst prompts a model to turn a stakeholder request into a user story, but the output keeps coming back as a loose paragraph with no acceptance criteria, breaking the team's backlog template. Which prompt change most directly fixes this?
choices: Specify the exact output template ('As a... I want... so that...' plus a numbered acceptance-criteria list) and show one example story in that format
Increase the temperature so the wording varies more each run
Ask the model to be 'a bit more structured' without giving a template
Re-send the request repeatedly until a well-formatted story appears
